messages: Hi, I'm preparing for a job interview next month for a product manager role. Can you help me plan my prep schedule and practice key questions?
I'd like a two-week plan that balances behavioral prep, product design exercises, and technical literacy. What would that look like day by day?
Could you give me a prioritized list of the most common behavioral questions PMs get and how to structure responses?
I've been told to use the STAR method, but I often run long. How can I condense STAR answers to about 90-120 seconds without losing impact?

Can you suggest three strong example stories I could use: leading a cross-functional project, resolving a major stakeholder conflict, and delivering under a tight deadline? I'll give details and you'll help shape them.

Here are the details for the first story: I led a feature launch with engineers and designers, about a three month timeline, and we had to pivot scope after beta feedback. How would I frame that as STAR?
For the stakeholder conflict: sales pushed for revenue-driving features, engineering warned of technical debt. I mediated and helped prioritize—how should I present my role and the outcome?
For the tight deadline story: I had to produce a working prototype in two weeks for a conference after the original plan fell through. What metrics and actions should I include to show impact?
Switching to product design exercises: what's a reliable framework to use for a 30-minute design interview on a mobile feature?
Can you give a sample outline for a 30-minute design answer: opening, scoping assumptions, user needs, success metrics, solution sketch, trade-offs, next steps?
What's a concise way to sketch flows on a whiteboard when time is limited—any shorthand, templates, or phrases I can rehearse?
On technical literacy: which topics should a PM at a mid-size SaaS company be comfortable with? I know basic SQL and APIs.
How deep should I go on system design? For PM interviews, are high-level trade-offs usually enough, or should I be ready with data models and simple architecture diagrams?
I also want to prep for product analytics questions—what are common scenarios and quick formulas or approaches I should be able to explain on the spot?
Could you help me craft a polished 30-second elevator pitch that communicates my product vision, impact, and what I bring to the team?
I plan to schedule five mock interviews: two behavioral, two design, and one hiring-manager fit session. What's an effective schedule and how should I structure feedback?
What are some smart questions I can ask interviewers that both demonstrate product thinking and help me evaluate the role?
Logistics question: for an on-site PM interview at a mid-size tech firm, is business casual safe, or should I lean more formal?
How should I plan travel and timing on interview day to minimize stress? Any simple checklist for what to bring?
What are good ways to close an interview during the last minute so I leave a strong impression?
How and when should I follow up after the interview? What's a concise thank-you message that restates interest and adds value?
On salary, if they ask current comp and I want to aim higher, what's a tactful way to respond that keeps negotiating leverage?
References: how should I prep my references and what should I tell them to expect if the hiring team calls?
Any quick breathing or mental prep techniques you recommend for calming nerves right before the interview?
Update: I just got a message from the recruiter with a big change to the format.

Instead of the in-person process we've been planning, they now want a single recorded submission where I answer behavioral prompts and demonstrate a short product walkthrough plus a light coding example.
The timeline changed too—the window to produce the recording is much shorter than I expected, so I'll need to prepare and record almost immediately.
That eliminates the possibility of an in-person whiteboard and means I must convey technical ability through screen capture only.
This really undercuts the two-week in-person rehearsal plan I was building, so a lot of my prep needs to be rethought.
I have a laptop with a webcam and screen-recording software and access to a sandbox repo, but no external camera or studio setup.
I can do basic edits—cuts, captions, splicing takes—but nothing elaborate, and the recruiters said the submission should be self-contained.
I need to be concise and credible in a single take or two, showing product thinking, communication, and some technical fluency.
Given all that, please help me reprioritize: what should I focus on immediately to make the most persuasive short submission?
Also, how should I repurpose the stories and design practice I already planned to fit this new format?
Given this pivot to a single compact recording made on my laptop, can you outline a minute-by-minute script for the video: which stories to open with, how to transition to the demo, and how to close?
How can I compress STAR answers even more so they hit the key beats quickly without sounding rushed or superficial?
For the screen-captured demo and simple code sample, what's the most reliable way to set up and present them so they appear polished and honest with basic tools?
What should I rehearse to perfection and what can I safely improvise during recording to keep the delivery natural under time pressure?
Any straightforward framing, lighting, and quick editing advice to make the result feel professional when I only have a laptop and one evening to produce it?
Lastly, what single sentence or moment should I use at the end to leave the strongest, most memorable impression in such a compact format?
